title: Brazil PPCDAm — Action Plan to Prevent & Control Legal Amazon Deforestation (2004)
text: A federal action plan combining expanded real-time satellite enforcement (DETER/IBAMA), a wave of new protected areas and indigenous lands, and restricted subsidized rural credit for municipalities with high deforestation. Not a single land-use law but a bundle of enforcement, land-tenure, and fiscal-credit levers aimed at halting illegal clearing across the Legal Amazon.
documentedOutcome: Brazilian Amazon annual primary-forest loss (PRODES) fell roughly 84%, from ~27,772 km2/yr in 2004 to ~4,571 km2/yr in 2012; econometric work attributes a large share to enforcement, protected-area creation, and the rural-credit restriction.

precomputedCounterfactual.cite: Assuncao, Gandour & Rocha (2015), Climate Policy Initiative / PUC-Rio
assumptions: non-random siting corrected in source study
leakage/SUTVA displacement not fully controlled
commodity-price co-drivers only partially controlled

title: Brazil Amazon enforcement rollback (2019-2022)
text: Reduced IBAMA enforcement budgets and fines, weakened environmental licensing, and signalled tolerance for land clearing in the Legal Amazon. Primarily an administrative and fiscal-economic reversal of the PPCDAm-era enforcement rather than an explicit land-use law — a natural inverse analog.
documentedOutcome: After enforcement was cut from 2019, PRODES primary-forest loss climbed from ~10,129 km2 (2019) to ~13,038 km2 (2021), the highest in 15 years; dry-season FIRMS fire counts and Sentinel-5P CO spiked over the same window.

precomputedCounterfactual.method: observed PRODES increase vs pre-rollback trend (inverse case; no matched DiD)
precomputedCounterfactual.cite: INPE PRODES annual estimates; MapBiomas Alerta
assumptions: counterfactual is the pre-2019 declining trend
COVID-era economic shifts co-vary
attribution to policy vs price is partial

title: Indonesia permanent peatland & forest moratorium (2016 -> permanent 2019)
text: Extended and later made permanent a moratorium on new concessions in primary forest and peatland, established the Peatland Restoration Agency (BRG), and blocked drainage of carbon-rich peat. Mixes land-use regulation with concession-economics and agricultural-subsidy levers.
documentedOutcome: Active-fire counts and aerosol optical depth over peat provinces fell sharply after 2016 versus the catastrophic 2015 haze crisis; primary-forest loss in Indonesia declined for several consecutive years after 2016 (Global Forest Watch).

precomputedCounterfactual.method: post-2016 loss-rate decline vs 2015 baseline (GFW; no matched DiD)
precomputedCounterfactual.cite: Global Forest Watch / WRI Indonesia primary-forest-loss series
assumptions: wet La Nina years reduced fire independent of policy
palm-oil price co-driver not controlled

title: Costa Rica Payments for Environmental Services (PES, 1997)
text: A national program paying landowners for forest conservation and reforestation, funded largely by a fuel tax and administered by FONAFIFO. A fiscal/market-incentive lever (payments + earmarked tax) rather than a command-and-control land-use ban.
documentedOutcome: National forest cover rebounded from ~40% in 1987 to over half the country by the 2010s under PES and complementary bans on land-use change — one of the clearest national reforestation reversals on record.

precomputedCounterfactual.method: parcel-matched avoided-deforestation studies find small-but-real additionality (published)
precomputedCounterfactual.cite: Daniels et al. (2010), Ecological Economics; Robalino & Pfaff (2013)
assumptions: much reforestation coincided with agricultural transition, not only PES
additionality of payments is modest in matched studies

title: China Grain-for-Green / Sloping Land Conversion Program (1999)
text: A subsidy program paying farmers in grain and cash to convert steeply sloping cropland to forest or grassland to curb erosion. A pure fiscal-subsidy lever with large land-cover consequences — cropland converted to tree/grass cover across the Loess Plateau.
documentedOutcome: The program converted on the order of 15 Mha of sloped cropland nationally; Loess Plateau vegetation cover and NDVI rose markedly, cutting sediment load to the Yellow River, though with documented soil-water and water-yield trade-offs.

precomputedCounterfactual.method: NDVI / vegetation-cover trend attribution (published; not avoided-loss framing)
precomputedCounterfactual.cite: Chen et al. (2015), Nature Climate Change; Feng et al. (2016), Nature Climate Change
assumptions: regional warming/CO2 fertilization co-drive greening
water-yield reductions are a real trade-off

title: Nepal Community Forestry (Forest Act 1993)
text: Devolved management of national forest to local Community Forest User Groups, giving communities rights and responsibilities over nearby forest. A land-tenure/governance lever rather than a spending program.
documentedOutcome: Handover to community user groups is associated with measurable canopy regrowth and increased forest density across the middle hills over subsequent decades.

assumptions: out-migration and remittances reduced fuelwood pressure concurrently

title: BC FireSmart & fuel-management funding (2018 -> )
text: Sustained provincial funding for community fuel-reduction, prescribed burns, and wildfire prevention. An economic/budget lever whose climate-adjacent effect is mediated through fuel loads and subsequent burn severity.
documentedOutcome: In treated wildland-urban-interface units, Landsat/Sentinel NBR burn severity is lower where fuel treatments preceded fire; treated-versus-untreated contrasts are visible in post-2021 season imagery.

precomputedCounterfactual.cite: BC Wildfire Service fuel-treatment evaluations; FireSmart Canada
assumptions: treatment siting is non-random (near communities)
weather dominates severity in extreme years

title: Ontario forest-management & conservation budget cuts (2019)
text: Reduced conservation-authority funding and forest-management program spending. A purely economic policy with no explicit climate language, but with downstream effects on fuel management and monitoring capacity — a non-obvious fiscal lever.
documentedOutcome: Reduced prescribed-burn and thinning activity correlates with higher modelled fuel accumulation; FIRMS shows sustained boreal fire activity, though attribution to the cuts alone is weak and is flagged as such.

precomputedCounterfactual.cite: Ontario Auditor General reports on forest management
assumptions: confounded by climate-driven fire-season lengthening
attribution to budget alone is weak

title: India Bhadla Solar Park build-out (Rajasthan, 2015-2019)
text: State-backed solar-energy policy and land allocation that converted arid scrub/sparse land into one of the world's largest solar parks. An energy/infrastructure policy that is a hidden land-cover lever: renewable build-out changes surface cover at landscape scale.
documentedOutcome: The Bhadla Solar Park (~2.2 GW, largely commissioned 2018-2019) converted roughly 57 km2 of arid Thar land into a contiguous built solar footprint — a large geometric change plainly visible in true-color imagery.

precomputedCounterfactual.method: land-cover conversion footprint (observed area; not avoided-loss)
precomputedCounterfactual.cite: IRENA / MNRE project documentation; Sentinel-2 true-color
assumptions: arid siting minimizes forest/cropland displacement here (not generalizable)

title: Ethiopia Tigray land restoration — exclosures & terracing (1991 -> )
text: Regional restoration combining community exclosures (protecting degraded hillslopes from grazing), mass mobilization for terracing, and area closures. A labor/governance and land-use lever aimed at reversing severe land degradation.
documentedOutcome: Community exclosures and terracing since the early 1990s greened degraded hillslopes; NDVI, vegetation cover, and biomass recovery are documented across Tigray, with restored spring flow in places.

precomputedCounterfactual.cite: Nyssen et al. (2015), Land Degradation & Development; Aerts et al. (2009)
assumptions: rainfall recovery co-varies with restoration
recent conflict (2020-22) may disturb the t1 signal
